name: Umayr ibn Wahb and Safwan ibn Umayyah
epithet: Two Cousins Who Came to Kill and Stayed to Believe
title: Umayr ibn Wahb and Safwan ibn Umayyah (RA): Two Cousins Who Came to Kill and Stayed to Believe
meta_description: The story of Umayr ibn Wahb and Safwan ibn Umayyah (RA), two cousins of Quraysh who plotted to kill the Prophet Muhammad and were guided to Islam instead. A study in how mercy outlasts hatred.
keywords: Umayr ibn Wahb, Safwan ibn Umayyah, story of Umayr ibn Wahb, conspiracy against the Prophet, Banu Jumah, conquest of Makkah, sahaba stories
lede: Two men sat in the courtyard of the Kaaba, grieving their dead and plotting a murder. One of them sharpened a sword, dipped it in poison, and set out for Madinah to kill the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He believed no living person could know what he had agreed to in secret.

This is the story of Umayr ibn Wahb and his cousin Safwan ibn Umayyah (may Allah be pleased with them): two proud nobles of Quraysh who came to the Prophet ﷺ as enemies, and were met with a patience that undid them.

paras: Umayr and Safwan belonged to Banu Jumah, one of the smaller but more distinguished clans of Makkah. They were known for their wealth and for a particular honour: they perfumed the idols and largely ran the fragrance trade of the city. The sweet scents of Makkah passed through their hands.

Their fathers were among the fiercest enemies of the Prophet ﷺ. Safwan's father, Umayyah ibn Khalaf, was the chief of the clan and the man who tortured Bilal (RA) in the open sun. From the same family came Ubayy ibn Khalaf, the only man ever to die at the hand of the Prophet ﷺ, after swearing he would kill him. This was the household Umayr and Safwan came from: wealthy, proud, and bound up in the opposition to Islam from the start.

These two were not young men chasing their fathers' approval. They were grown, with wives and children old enough to fight. The hostility toward the Prophet ﷺ was not borrowed. It was theirs, held with conviction, and they were proud of it.

sections.h2: The day two armies met two armies
paras: At Badr, Umayr was sent ahead to scout the Muslim camp. He counted close to three hundred men, every horse, every shield, and reported back with precision. But something unsettled him. He told the chiefs of Quraysh he had a bad feeling, that the faith he saw in those outnumbered men did not look right, and that they should turn back. Abu Jahl mocked him, and the army marched.

There is a verse that the Prophet's companions later understood through men like these. The Muslims at Badr saw the angels descend to help them. On the other side, Satan appeared in a familiar form, urging the disbelievers forward, promising that no one could defeat them. Then, when the two forces came into view, he fled.

Umayr would tell this story himself, years later, as a believer. While the two armies faced each other, another pair of armies was meeting unseen: the angels and the forces of Satan. When Satan saw them, he turned and ran.

ayah.text: Satan made their foul deeds seem fair to them, and said, ‘No one will conquer you today, for I will be right beside you,’ but when the armies came within sight of one another he turned on his heels, saying, ‘This is where I leave you: I see what you do not, and I fear God- God is severe in His punishment.’

sections.h2: A man who woke among the dead
paras: Badr went badly for Quraysh. Umayr was knocked from his horse and a Muslim drove a sword through him, so deep that its tip struck the stones beneath him. The man left him for dead.

Night fell. Umayr woke in the desert, surrounded by seventy of his own people who would never wake again. The sword had passed through him, yet somehow he had not died. He rose from among the bodies, made his way back to Makkah, and slowly healed.

It is hard to imagine the state of a man given back his life like that. But he did not yet read it as mercy. He read it as a reason for revenge, and he carried that bitterness home to a city drowning in grief.

sections.h2: The plot in the courtyard of the Kaaba
paras: Every home in Makkah had lost someone at Badr, and the city was heavy with grief and the wish for revenge. Safwan, now among the richest men in Makkah, had watched his own father die at the hands of the very man he used to torture. He had all the wealth in the world and could not buy back a single thing he had lost. That, the historians note, frustrated him most of all.

One day Umayr and Safwan sat together near the Kaaba, walking and talking to pass the weight of those days. They spoke of the friends and fathers and uncles who had been killed. Umayr let his tongue slip. He said that were it not for a debt he could not pay and a family he would leave behind, he would ride to Madinah and kill the Prophet ﷺ himself. Safwan saw his opening. He promised to clear every debt and care for Umayr's family for the rest of their days. Now there was no excuse left.

They agreed it would stay between the two of them. Umayr took a sword, had it dipped in poison, concealed it, and set out for Madinah, certain he would die in the attempt and not caring, as long as the Prophet ﷺ died first.

sections.h2: The conversation no one had heard
paras: Umayr reached the Prophet's mosque. Umar ibn al-Khattab (RA) saw him at the door, sword hanging from his neck, and knew at once that the man had come with harm in his heart. He seized Umayr by that very sword and dragged him in. The Prophet ﷺ told him to let go.

Umayr greeted him in the manner of the days of ignorance, and the Prophet ﷺ told him gently that Allah had given the believers a better greeting: peace. Then he asked why Umayr had come. Umayr offered his excuse: he had come to ransom his captured son. The Prophet ﷺ asked him twice more for the truth, and twice more Umayr repeated the same story. Then the Prophet ﷺ told it back to him. He recounted the conversation in the courtyard, word for word: the dead they had mourned, the vow Umayr had made, the promise Safwan had given in return.

There was no way anyone could have carried those words to Madinah in time, and no way anyone could have heard them at all. Umayr understood. He said there had been no one present but himself and Safwan, and that only Allah could have brought this to the Prophet ﷺ. He took the testimony of faith on the spot. The Prophet ﷺ returned his son and told him to teach his household this religion.

sections.h2: The cloak that brought Safwan back
paras: Umayr asked to return to Makkah, into the same anger he had left, to call his own people to Islam. He guided a number of them quietly and, on his second journey to Madinah, arrived with a small caravan of new Muslims, reciting Quran on the back of his camel. His son grew into a companion who helped open Egypt and was martyred at Yarmuk.

Safwan was harder. He had sworn that if every last person of Quraysh accepted Islam, he would be the one who never did. When Makkah was finally conquered, he fled toward the sea, meaning to sail to Yemen. Umayr asked the Prophet ﷺ for a chance to bring him back, and the Prophet ﷺ handed him his own cloak as a sign of safety.

Umayr caught Safwan at the water's edge. Safwan did not believe him until he saw the cloak. He returned, but on his own terms, asking for time to consider the message. The Prophet ﷺ gave him longer than he asked for. He even fought alongside the Muslims at Hunayn while still a disbeliever, lending his stored weapons on the promise they would be returned. Afterward the Prophet ﷺ gave him herd after herd of camels until Safwan said no heart could be this generous except a prophet's. His own words: he was given all of that while the Prophet ﷺ was the most hated man on earth to him, and the giving continued until the Prophet ﷺ became the most beloved. Then he believed.

sections.h2: How completely a heart can turn
paras: What is striking is not only that the two men believed, but how completely they changed. Umar, who had wanted to break Umayr's neck at the door, later said that there had been a time when no person on earth was more hateful to him than Umayr, and that now Umayr was more beloved to him than his own children.

Umayr never wanted to stay safe in Madinah. He asked to go back into the anger of Makkah precisely because he had once fought so hard against the faith, and he wanted to undo some of that harm by calling others to it. He spent the rest of his life striving in that path.

Safwan, once he believed, did not hold back either. He became known for a generosity so open that any hungry person in Makkah or Madinah knew they could come to his door and be fed. He had sworn he would be the last man alive to follow this religion. He ended his life feeding the poor in its name.

lessons_heading: What these two lives teach us
lessons_intro: Their story is not really about a foiled assassination. It is about how patiently mercy can wait for a hard heart, and how completely a heart can turn once it gives way.

lessons.text: One man came carrying a poisoned sword; the other swore he would be the last holdout alive. Both became companions. It is a warning against ever writing a person off, including ourselves.

lessons.bold: Mercy can disarm what force cannot.
lessons.text: The Prophet ﷺ did not answer the plot with punishment. He answered it with the truth, gently spoken, and with a generosity that wore the hatred down.

lessons.bold: Be slow to decide who is your enemy.
lessons.text: Umar wanted to strike Umayr down. Days later they were brothers studying the Quran together. The person in front of you today is not fixed forever.

lessons.bold: A second chance is meant to change you.
lessons.text: Umayr woke among the dead and first wanted revenge. The same survival, read again, became the door to his faith. What we make of our mercies is up to us.

faq.q: Who were Umayr ibn Wahb and Safwan ibn Umayyah?
faq.a: Two cousins from the clan of Banu Jumah in Makkah, both early enemies of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Umayr set out to assassinate him and was guided to Islam instead. Safwan, one of the wealthiest men of Makkah, held out far longer before accepting faith.

faq.q: How was the plot against the Prophet ﷺ discovered?
faq.a: Umayr and Safwan had agreed in secret, with no one else present. When Umayr arrived in Madinah with a hidden poisoned sword, the Prophet ﷺ repeated their private conversation back to him word for word. Umayr understood that only Allah could have revealed it, and he accepted Islam.

faq.q: Why did Safwan take so long to accept Islam?
faq.a: Safwan was deeply tied to the leadership of Quraysh and had lost his father at Badr. He swore he would never follow this religion. He returned to Makkah under the Prophet's protection, asked for time, and only believed after the Prophet's repeated generosity softened his heart.

faq.q: What can we learn from their story?
faq.a: That no heart is beyond guidance, that patience and mercy can change an enemy where force never could, and that we should be slow to decide who is permanently against us.

closing_heading: Why this story stays with us
closing: It is easy to keep a private list of people we have quietly given up on. The relative who wronged us. The person whose mind we are sure will never change. Our own selves, on the days we feel too far gone. The story of these two cousins is a gentle argument against every name on that list.

One arrived with poison in his hand and left as a teacher of the faith. The other swore he would be the last man on earth to believe, and still his heart turned. Today, that asks something small and difficult of us: to meet a hard heart with patience instead of a verdict, to keep a door open one more day, and to trust that hearts belong to Allah, who can turn them when we have stopped expecting it.
source_note: The narrative follows the account of Umayr ibn Wahb and Safwan ibn Umayyah (RA) in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts (Yaqeen Institute). The Quran translation is from The Qur'an, M.A.S. Abdel Haleem (Quran 8:48). Where the historical record carries more than one narration, we have followed the most widely reported.

slides:
Two cousins sat by the Kaaba, grieving their dead.

They agreed on a murder.

No one else heard a word of it.

One of them sharpened a sword, dipped it in poison, and rode for Madinah to kill the Prophet ﷺ.

He was certain his secret was safe.

He had survived Badr by a miracle.

A sword ran through him; he woke at night among seventy of his own dead and somehow lived.

He read it as a reason for revenge.

At the mosque, Umar (RA) saw the hidden sword and dragged him in.

The man gave a false excuse. Twice.

Then the Prophet ﷺ told him the truth.

Word for word, the Prophet ﷺ repeated the secret conversation.

No one had been there but the two cousins.

Umayr said: only Allah could have told you this. And he believed.

The other cousin, Safwan, swore he would be the last man alive to ever follow this religion.

He ran for the sea rather than submit.

The Prophet ﷺ sent his own cloak as a sign of safety.

Then gave Safwan herd after herd of camels.

Safwan said: no heart could be this generous but a prophet's. And he believed.

**No one is past saving.**

One came to kill. One swore he never would. Both died as companions.

May Allah be pleased with them.
social.caption:
Two cousins of Quraysh, Umayr ibn Wahb and Safwan ibn Umayyah (may Allah be pleased with them), once plotted the death of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ.

Umayr came to Madinah with a poisoned sword, sure no one knew his secret. The Prophet ﷺ repeated the plot back to him word for word, and Umayr's heart broke open. Safwan held out far longer, swearing he would be the last to ever believe, until a patience and generosity he could not explain finally undid him.

Neither of them was beyond guidance. Maybe no one is.

Which part of their story stayed with you? 🤍

Save this for later, and share it with someone who would love it.

Read the full story: buruja.com/companions/a-foiled-conspiracy

email.subject: They came to kill him, and stayed to believe
email.alt_subject: The plot only two men knew about
email.preview: A poisoned sword, a secret no one had heard, and a patience that undid two enemies.
email.body:
Assalamu alaikum,

Two cousins of Quraysh, Umayr ibn Wahb and Safwan ibn Umayyah (may Allah be pleased with them), once sat together near the Kaaba, grieving the dead of Badr and plotting a murder.

Umayr said that were it not for his debts and his family, he would ride to Madinah and kill the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ himself. Safwan, one of the richest men in Makkah, promised to clear every debt and care for his family. Now there was no excuse left. They agreed it would stay between the two of them.

Umayr took a sword, had it dipped in poison, and set out, certain he would die in the attempt and not caring, as long as the Prophet ﷺ died first.

When he reached the mosque, Umar ibn al-Khattab saw the hidden sword and dragged him in. Umayr gave a false reason for coming. The Prophet ﷺ asked twice more for the truth, and twice more Umayr repeated the same story.

Then the Prophet ﷺ told it back to him. He recounted the conversation in the courtyard, word for word: the dead they had mourned, the vow Umayr had made, the promise Safwan had given. No one had heard those words but the two of them.

Umayr understood at once. Only Allah could have brought this to him. He accepted Islam there and then, and later returned to Makkah to call his own people to the faith.

Safwan was harder. He had sworn that if every last person of Quraysh believed, he would be the one who never did. When Makkah was conquered, he fled toward the sea. The Prophet ﷺ sent his own cloak as a sign of safety, and gave him herd after herd of camels, until Safwan said no heart could be this generous except a prophet's. Then he believed too.

There is a quiet lesson in these two: no one is past saving. Not the relative who wronged us, not the person we are sure will never change, not even ourselves on the days we feel too far gone.

May Allah be pleased with them both, and soften our hearts the way He softened theirs.

If you would like the full story, we have written it here:

paras: Two cousins sat in the courtyard of the Kaaba, grieving their dead and plotting a murder. One of them, Umayr ibn Wahb, sharpened a sword, dipped it in poison, and set out for Madinah to kill the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. He believed no living person could know what he had agreed to in secret.

Umayr and Safwan ibn Umayyah (may Allah be pleased with them) belonged to a proud clan of Makkah and had inherited their fathers' enmity toward the Prophet ﷺ. At the battle of Badr, Umayr had been struck down and left for dead. He woke at night among seventy of his own people who would never wake again, and somehow he had survived. He carried that survival home not as mercy, but as a reason for revenge.
So when Safwan promised to clear his debts and care for his family, Umayr had no excuse left. He concealed the poisoned sword and rode for Madinah, ready to die as long as the Prophet ﷺ died first.

At the mosque, Umar ibn al-Khattab saw the hidden sword and dragged him in. Umayr gave a false reason for coming. The Prophet ﷺ asked twice more for the truth, and twice more Umayr lied. Then the Prophet ﷺ told the story back to him, word for word: the courtyard, the dead they had mourned, the vow, the promise. No one had heard those words but the two cousins.
Umayr understood. "Only Allah could have told you this," he said, and he believed. He went back to Makkah to call his own people to the faith, and returned reciting Quran on the back of his camel.

Safwan was harder. He had sworn he would be the last man alive to follow this religion, and when Makkah fell he fled toward the sea. The Prophet ﷺ sent his own cloak as a sign of safety, then gave Safwan herd after herd of camels, until Safwan said no heart could be this generous except a prophet's. Then his heart, too, gave way.
storybook.pullquote: Only Allah could have told you this.
storybook.reflection: It is easy to keep a private list of people we have given up on, and to put our own name on it on the hard days. These two cousins are a gentle argument against every name on that list. One came with poison in his hand; the other swore he never would. Both were met with patience instead of a verdict, and both hearts turned. Hearts belong to Allah, who can turn them long after we have stopped expecting it.
storybook.source: Their story follows the account in Dr. Omar Suleiman's series The Firsts.
